coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of quantum dots based on microfluidic technology
coredata.prism:issueName: Reaction Engineering and Catalysis * Nanotechnology: Nanofluidics and Microfluidics
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Guo-Xing
Li, Qing
Cheng, Rui
Chen, Su
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, quantum dots (QDs) have attracted considerable research interests in the fields of biomedicine, energy, optoelectronics, and photocatalysis owing to their unmatched luminescent properties, excellent fluorescence stability, and relatively low cost. Microfluidic technology for the synthesis of QDs has shown unparalleled superiority as a result of its simple operation, fast heat and mass transfer, and precise control of reaction parameters. In this review, we will focus on the application of microfluidic platforms in the synthesis of single core and core-shell semiconductor QDs, perovskite QDs (PQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). We do expect that this review will provide new insights for the fabrication of various QDs and nanomaterials by microfluidic reaction platforms.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microfluidics in fabrication of semiconductor QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Microfluidics used to synthesize single-core QDs. (a) I) Schematic diagram of the droplet-based microfluidic device and II) process of synthesizing water-soluble Ag2S QDs [23]. (b) Schematic illustration of ultrasonic-microfluidic technology for stripping of BP powers into QDs [25• ].

(a) Schematic diagram of ZnSe/ZnS core-shell QDs fabricated using microfluidic reaction system, which consists of three parts: inlet, mixing zone and heating zone [30]. (b) Schematic of a two-stage microreactor for the synthesis of CuInS2/ZnS QDs [31]. (c) I) Multi-stage microreactor for synthesizing various core-shell QDs. II) Microreactor with 10 subchannels ensuring ZnS shell formation [33•• ].

(a) Illustration of the droplet-based microfluidic platform integrated with online absorbance and fluorescence detection for the synthesis and real time characterization of CsPbX3 perovskite QDs [38]. (b) Illustration of the segmented-flow reaction platform equipped with online PL and absorbance modules for the synthesis and realtime monitoring of CsxFA1−xPbX3 perovskite QDs [41•• ]. (c) Schematic illustration of the automated and modular microfluidic platform with the three-port translational flow cell and the optional extension module [42].

(a) I) Schematic diagram of the fabrication of CQDs using a multilayer microfluidic chip. II) Optical photograph of this microfluidic platform [46]. (b) Microreactors with different microchannels (Linear-like, Double-snake-like, Snake-like) for fabrication of CQDs [47]. (c) Microreactor and microchannel reaction region with foamy copper for synthesis of high-performance CQDs [48•• ]. (d) Microreactor with porous copper fibers for synthesizing N-CDs [49].

head.ce:title: Synthesis of quantum dots based on microfluidic technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials, Nanjing Tech University (Former Nanjing University of Technology), Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering
College of Chemical Engineering, and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals and Functional Polymer Materials
Nanjing Tech University (Former Nanjing University of Technology)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, quantum dots (QDs) have attracted considerable research interests in the fields of biomedicine, energy, optoelectronics, and photocatalysis owing to their unmatched luminescent properties, excellent fluorescence stability, and relatively low cost. Microfluidic technology for the synthesis of QDs has shown unparalleled superiority as a result of its simple operation, fast heat and mass transfer, and precise control of reaction parameters. In this review, we will focus on the application of microfluidic platforms in the synthesis of single core and core-shell semiconductor QDs, perovskite QDs (PQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). We do expect that this review will provide new insights for the fabrication of various QDs and nanomaterials by microfluidic reaction platforms.

ce:sections.ce:para: This review comes from a themed issue on Nanotechnology: nanofluidics and microfluidics
For a complete overview see the Issue and the Editorial

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) have attracted tremendous interests due to their fascinating properties such as size effect, quantum confinement effect, surface effect [1]. These unique features render them distinct advantages for various applications in the fields of light-emitting devices [2,3], life science research [4], solar cells [5,6] and fingerprint detection [7,8]. Considerable efforts have been made to synthesize QDs, where two main approaches are employed, namely organic phase preparation and aqueous phase preparation. However, tough experimental conditions, complicated operation and low yield remain key challenges, which greatly limit their applications in various fields. Therefore, a facile and efficient method is encouraged to prepare high-performance QDs.

The concept of microfluidics was first put forward by Manz in the 1990s [9]. With the development of micromachining, microfluidic technology has been developed rapidly, which have made strides in various fields. Compared with the traditional chemical synthesis method, the microfluidic technology shows several distinct advantages [10–12]. I) Precise control of the unit operation process can be achieved by regulating the geometric size morphology of the microchannel, as well as the interface properties and chemical properties of the fluid. II) Microfluidics can realize rapid reaction in a continuous manner, which is promising in large-scale production. III) The homogeneous mixing/reacting/self-assembling process in the confined microchannel guarantees the superior properties of the product, such as controllable morphology and good homogeneity of size. IV) Micro-nano composite particles can be easily prepared through coupling multi-step synthesis process. Because of these unique advantages, microfluidic technology has been widely applied in the fields of chemistry [13], physics [14] and biology [15,16], especially in the synthesis of nanomaterials [17,18], which has opened up a new field.

Microfluidics hold the promise for green synthesis and continuous preparation of QDs, and many researchers have made great contributions in this respect. In fact, the chip plays a key role in the fabrication of nanomaterials, which have made significant strides both in chip configuration and chip technology [19]. In this paper, we review the research progresses in the preparation of various QDs by microfluidic technology, and emphatically expound the diversification of the chips design and the current research status of using microfluidic technology to prepare single-core and core-shell semiconductor QDs, perovskite QDs (PQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). It is expected that this review can provide guidance for the further design and preparation of diversified QDs in a facile, continuous and large-scale fashion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microfluidics in fabrication of semiconductor QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor QDs, a class of mainly quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials composed of II-VI elements or III-V elements, have been widely investigated in recent years. When it comes to the fabrication of QDs by microfluidics, some progresses and achievements have been made. In 2002, Edel et al. [20] firstly proposed a method for preparing CdS QDs using a continuous flow microreactor. By changing the flow rate of the precursors in the microchannels, nanoparticles with different sizes were finally synthesized. Since then, more and more researchers have employed this technology to synthesize QDs. Tian et al. [21] prepared CdSe nanocrystals by using a microfluidic platform. In this system, they used dodecylamine and olamine as ligands and separated the nucleation and growth stages through adjusting the mixing ratio of the two ligands and the reaction temperature. Finally, full-color luminescent CdSe nanocrystals with good monodispersity and narrow half peak width were obtained. Subsequently, they designed another microfluidic system [22], where the precursors were firstly preheated. Compared with the former method, this modified strategy could consume a large amount of precursors in the nucleation stage, which was beneficial to the synthesis of CdSe QDs with smaller size.

High-performance QDs are highly desired to satisfy their vigorous advancements in the practical applications. To this end, many researchers have successfully synthesized core-shell QDs to overcome defects by surface modification, in which the core is wrapped in another lattice matching and wide-band gap shell [28,29]. Up to now, considerable efforts have been made to achieve core-shell QDs via microfluidics, where complex chips and multi-stage microfluidic reactors were utilized. In 2012, Kwon et al. [30] designed a microfluidic system (Figure 2 a) for in-situ synthesis of ZnSe/ZnS QDs, which consists of three parts: inlet, mixing zone and heating zone, giving rise to a three-stage reaction processes, namely homogenous mixing of precursors, formation and coating of ZnSe QDs. In addition, lower reaction temperature is enough for the synthesis of QDs, because the heat transfer efficiency of the microchannel is higher than that of the traditional batch systems. deMello’s group utilized a two-stage microfluidic reactor for synthesis of CuInS2/ZnS QDs (Figure 2b) [31]. Interestingly, since the microfluidic platform does not need to passivate the ZnS, the single source precursor of ZnS can be directly injected into the droplet containing CuInS2 to rapidly synthesize the core-shell CuInS2/ZnS QDs. Besides, the platform can also realize independent control of each reaction process, and quickly adjust the reaction parameters involved in the synthesis process to achieve optimal reaction conditions. Tian et al. [32] also successfully synthesized the CuInS2/ZnS core-shell QDs by assembling the commercially available, higher-capacity micro-reaction modules into experimental devices. It should be noted that the output is far larger than that reported, which opens up new avenues for the industrialization of nanocrystals.

Baek et al. [33•• ] recently reported a multi-stage microfluidic reactor with six reaction platforms (Figure 2c-I), which can be easily and quickly adjusted by reassembling different reaction platforms to synthesize various III-V based core/shell QDs in the optimal reaction conditions, such as InP/ZnS, InP/ZnSe, InP/CdS, and InAs/InP. The shell reactor owns 10 subchannels (Figure 2c-II), thus ensuring a lower concentration of the shell precursor and preventing secondary nucleation. In this case, the finally prepared core-shell QDs have a narrow half-peak width distribution and a high QY value. More importantly, this method can greatly shorten the total reaction time. Therefore, such microfluidic devices provide multi-step reaction platforms for core-shell QDs synthesis, which offer flexibility for optimizing reaction conditions, high efficiency for short reaction time, especially great potentials for regulation of high-performance QDs and mechanism investigation.

In recent years, CQDs have attracted both enormous academic and industrial interests for its fantastic features including biocompatibility, low toxicity and cost, excellent photostability and tunable fluorescence emission [43,44]. Currently, synthesis of CQDs via microfluidics have been still in its infancy and rare examples have been reported. Lu et al. [45] assembled polytetrafluoroethylene microtubes and micro-mixer to form a brief micro-reaction system and successfully realized the synthesis of CQDs based on the thermal decomposition of organic carbon sources. In addition, they also investigated the effects of various reaction conditions (carbon source, reactant concentration, reaction time and temperature) on the CQDs synthesis process. This study can be recognized as the first example to report microfluidics as an effective tool for CQDs synthesis. However, whether the PTFE microtubes in this study can be used as a chip system in microfluidic technology is still debatable.

In the same year, Pedro et al. [46] reported the CQDs synthesis in a multilayer structure chip system (Figure 4 a) with ascorbic acid as the carbon source and dimethyl sulfoxide as the solvent. CQDs with a diameter of 3.3 nm and a QY of 2.6% were synthesized. This microfluidic system can realize precise control of the entire reaction process, including sample delivery and temperature, which greatly simplifies the operation process and gives new insight in CQDs fabrication.

Rao et al. [47] designed three types of microreactors (linear-like, double-snake-like, snake-like) by respectively integrating microfluidic chips with different microchannels to achieve rapid and continuous synthesis of CQDs (Figure 4b), where the linear-like microreactors showed the best results with QY up to 60.1%. In virtue of this microfluidic system, they proved that the photoluminescence intensity of CQDs mainly comes from its surface state, especially nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups, rather than size. Subsequently, the research team designed a foamy copper microreactor [48•• ] (Figure 4c), which used the tiny microchannels of the foamed copper itself to achieve a liquid-to-gas transition. It should be mentioned that this microreactor was conducive to the nucleation and growth of CQDs, leading to a high QY of 84.1%, which was significantly higher than that of the conventional hydrothermal method. They also developed a porous copper fiber microreactor for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CDs) (Figure 4d) [49]. Through continuously optimizing the synthesis conditions, such as reaction temperature, fluid flow rate, and porosity of copper fiber, they obtained N-CDs with QY of 73% and proved that rough copper fiber was beneficial to higher QY than that of smooth ones. These explorations have made indispensable progress in the fabrication of CQDs via microfluidic technology, which will spark the new synthesis process of CQDs in a rapid, flexible and continuous fashion.

During the last decades, microfluidic technology has been proved to be an efficient, flexible, simple and powerful pathway for the synthesis of high-performance nanomaterials. In terms of the synthesis of QDs, researchers have long endeavored to develop new microfluidic chips for various QDs, as well as explore the relevant mechanism. Compared with the traditional batch synthesis methods, microfluidics offer a series of advantages. Firstly, microfluidic systems can enable the fabrication of QDs in a microchip, giving rise to a fast mass and heat transfer, which greatly improve the reaction efficiency. In addition, precise control over reaction conditions can be easily realized in the microfluidics, which can guarantee the desirable morphology and uniform size. Moreover, microfluidics hold the potentials for large-scale production of QDs due to the characteristics of continuous operation and the parallel amplification. Finally, through coupling multi-step synthesis process, a variety of particles with desired functionality and diversity can be generated.

We believe that the majority of future research interests will be focused on the high-performance QDs and large-scale production, design of complex microfluidic system and green chemical synthesis. Synthesis of QDs with excellent properties through microfluidics is still on the way, where great efforts should be devoted to the precise design of chip, as well as precise control of a series of reaction conditions (temperature gradient, concentration gradient and flow rates etc.). This is of great significance since there are tremendous opportunities to stimulate the translation form the basic research to industrial production. When that happens, it will surely be a revolution in the field of nanomaterials. Integration of microfluidic system with automatic control and real-time analysis and detection is an inevitable trend of the future development of microfluidic technology. It is expected that smart microfluidic system will come out through integration of various components, which is favorable for understanding the synthesis mechanism, as well as achieving high-performance QDs. One critical challenge is to realize the effective integration of different modules, which enables the real-time monitoring, accurate analysis and detection, thus leading to the controllable synthesis of satisfactory products. Another attractive area will be focused on the green chemical synthesis, namely realizing the solvent safety, low-cost energy, pollution-free and high yield of products through the programmed microfluidic technology. A point shall be highlighted here is that the synthesis of CQDs is still in the infancy stage. There is still a long way to go in the future. The main reason is that the synthesis conditions of CQDs need to be in high temperature environments, so the requirements for the microfluidic system are relatively harsh. Besides, surface modification, passivation, and purification of CQDs also make it different for CQDs synthesis via microfluidics.

In general, microfluidic technology has demonstrated its advantages in the field of QDs synthesis, along with some critical challenges. With the development of microfluidic technology, these issues will be gradually resolved and great breakthroughs will be made in this field, which will provide guiding significance for nanomaterial synthesis, or even change the trajectory of the traditional production process.

Papers of particular interest, published within the period of review, have been highlighted as:
• of special interest
•• of outstanding interest

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21736006, 21908103), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFB0401700 and 2018YFC1602800), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190672), Fund of State Key Laboratory of Material-Oriented Chemical Engineering (ZK201704), and Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots and their multimodal applications: a review
53% Efficient red emissive carbon quantum dots for high color rendering and stable warm white-light-emitting diodes
Interfacial fabrication of single-crystalline ZnTe nanorods with high blue fluorescence
Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Alloying strategy in Cu-In-Ga-Se quantum dots for high efficiency quantum dot sensitized solar cells
Interface engineering of highly efficient perovskite solar cells
Red-emissive carbon dots for fingerprints detection by spray method: coffee ring effect and unquenched fluorescence in drying process
Recognition of latent fingerprints and ink-free printing derived from interfacial segregation of carbon dots
Micromachining of monocrystalline silicon and glass for chemical analysis systems. A look into next century’s technology or just a fashionable craze?
Microfluidic generation of magnetoresponsive Janus photonic crystal particles
Microfluidic synthesis of rigid nanovesicles for hydrophilic reagents delivery
Multifunctional micro/nanoscale fibers based on microfluidic spinning technology
Control and detection of chemical reactions in microfluidic systems
Physics and applications of microfluidics in biology
Future lab-on-a-chip technologies for interrogating individual molecules
Point-of-care biochemical assays using gold nanoparticle-implemented microfluidics
Bioinspired multifunctional spindle-knotted microfibers from microfluidics
A review on microreactors: reactor fabrication, design, and cutting-edge applications
Microfluidic routes to the controlled production of nanoparticles
Microfluidic synthesis of monodispersed CdSe quantum dots nanocrystals by using mixed fatty amines as ligands
Intensification of nucleation stage for synthesizing high quality CdSe quantum dots by using preheated precursors in microfluidic devices
Droplet-based microreactor for synthesis of water-soluble Ag2S quantum dots
High quantum yield ZnO quantum dots synthesizing via an ultrasonication microreactor method
Highly efficient and continuous production of few-layer black phosphorus nanosheets and quantum dots via acoustic-microfluidic process

ce:note.ce:simple-para: Ultrasonic-microfluidic technology provides a simple way for stripping of black phosphorus powder into QDs, which can guarantee better stripping efficiency with wide applicability in other two-dimensional materials.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microfluidic technology: uncovering the mechanisms of nanocrystal nucleation and growth

ce:note.ce:simple-para: This paper reported the uncovering mechanisms of nanocrystal nucleation and growth based on microfluidic technology.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Millisecond-timescale monitoring of PbS nanoparticle nucleation and growth using droplet-based microfluidics
Facile synthesis of Cu−In−S/ZnS Core/Shell quantum dots in 1-Dodecanethiol for efficient light-emitting diodes with an external quantum efficiency of 7.8%
Ideal CdSe/CdS core/shell nanocrystals enabled by entropic ligands and their core size-, shell thickness-, and ligand-dependent photoluminescence properties
Continuous in situ synthesis of ZnSe/ZnS Core/Shell quantum dots in a microfluidic reaction system and its application for light-emitting diodes
Scalable production of CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots in a two-step droplet-based microfluidic platform
Reaction engineering studies of the continuous synthesis of CuInS2 and CuInS2/ZnS nanocrystals
Multistage microfluidic platform for the continuous synthesis of III-V core/shell quantum dots

ce:note.ce:simple-para: A multi-stage microfluidic reactor can reassemble for synthesizing various QDs with a narrow half-peak width distribution and a high quantum yield, as well as largely shorten the total reaction time.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Perovskite quantum dots with near unity solution and neat-film photoluminescent quantum yield by novel spray synthesis
Aluminum-doped cesium lead bromide perovskite nanocrystals with stable blue photoluminescence used for display backlight
In situ fabrication of halide perovskite nanocrystals embedded in polymer composites via microfluidic spinning microreactors
Monodisperse formamidinium lead bromide nanocrystals with bright and stable green photoluminescence
Synthesis of cesium lead halide perovskite nanocrystals in a droplet-based microfluidic platform: fast parametric space mapping
Microfluidic reactors provide preparative and mechanistic insights into the synthesis of formamidinium lead halide perovskite nanocrystals
Unveiling the shape evolution and halide-ion-segregation in blue-emitting formamidinium lead halide perovskite nanocrystals using an automated microfluidic platform
xploration of near-infrared-emissive colloidal multinary lead halide perovskite nanocrystals using an automated microfluidic platform

ce:note.ce:simple-para: An automated microfluidic platform is used to synthesize PQDs, which can realize the precise synthesis owing to the thorough screening of reaction parameters and the ratio of precursors.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Automated microfluidic platform for systematic studies of colloidal perovskite nanocrystals: towards continuous nano-manufacturing
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Rapid and large-scale production of multi-fluorescence carbon dots via magnetic hyperthermia method
The use of a microreactor for rapid screening of the reaction conditions and investigation of the photoluminescence mechanism of carbon dots
Microsystem-assisted synthesis of carbon dots with fluorescent and colorimetric properties for pH detection
Efficient synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots by microreactor method and their application in Fe3+ ion detection
Rapid synthesis of highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots via a microreactor with foamy copper for the detection of Hg2+ ions

ce:note.ce:simple-para: A foamy copper microreactor was conducive to the nucleation and growth of CQDs, leading to a high QY of 84.1%.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent and stable N-Doped carbon dots as nanoprobes for Hg2+ detection
